text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum under the inferior mesenteric vein and performs blunt dissection to separate the space between the mesocolon and prurinal fat tissue. The lymph node dissection at the root of the inferior mesenteric vein is highlighted as important due to the lymph flow pattern of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection beneath the inferior mesenteric artery, exposing the origin of the left colic artery. A central tie is applied to the left colic artery.
The surgeon isolates and divides the inferior mesenteric vein below the inferior border of the pancreas, completing the division of this vascular structure.
The surgeon incises the lateral attachments of the colon up to the splenic flexure and then divides the frenocolic ligament.

The surgeon performs an oncologic dissection of the splenic flexure using an anterior approach. The greater omentum is divided close to the stomach, and the dissection continues leftward until the splenocolic ligament is divided. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, with the patient in the anti-Trendelenburg position and the cameraman positioned between the patient's legs using a thirty-degree camera angle.

The surgeon performs dissection of the left transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreas, dividing the pancreatic ligament. The final separation of the mesocolon from the spleen and retroperitoneum is conducted using diathermy.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic vessels and divides the left branch. A supraumbilical laparotomy incision is made through the umbilical port to remove the specimen. The marginal artery is divided in its transverse and left colonic portion. A GIA device is used to divide the large intestine, followed by a hands-on lateral-lateral colicolic anastomosis, completing the final specimen removal.
